subject: Physics
q: A man walks along an inclined plane at a uniform speed of $5\,\text{ms}^{-1}$. The plane is inclined at $37^\circ$ to the horizontal. While moving down the plane, he observes that the rain appears to fall straight vertically downward. When he moves up the same plane with the same speed, he observes that the rain appears to make an angle $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{8}\right)$ with the horizontal. The speed of the rain is
question: A man walks along an inclined plane at a uniform speed of $5\,\text{ms}^{-1}$. The plane is inclined at $37^\circ$ to the horizontal. While moving down the plane, he observes that the rain appears to fall straight vertically downward. When he moves up the same plane with the same speed, he observes that the rain appears to make an angle $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{8}\right)$ with the horizontal. The speed of the rain is
solution:
Let the horizontal ground direction be along the positive x -axis ( $\hat{\mathrm{i}}$ ) and the vertical upward direction be along the positive y -axis ( $\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ ).

Let the actual velocity vector of the rain with respect to the ground $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{r}}\right)$ be written as:

$$ \vec{v}_r=v_x \hat{\imath}+v_y \hat{\jmath} $$

Case 1 when man is moving down the inclined plane

The man moves down a $37^{\circ}$ incline at a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

Let the horizontal ground direction be along the positive x -axis ( $\hat{\mathrm{i}}$ ) and the vertical upward direction be along the positive y -axis ( $\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ ).

Let the actual velocity vector of the rain with respect to the ground $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{r}}\right)$ be written as:

$$ \vec{v}_r=v_x \hat{\imath}+v_y \hat{\jmath} $$

Case 1 when man is moving down the inclined plane

The man moves down a $37^{\circ}$ incline at a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

Resolving the man's velocity vector ($\vec{v}_{m1}$) into horizontal and vertical components:

$$\vec{v}_{m1} = (5 \cos 37^\circ)\hat{i} - (5 \sin 37^\circ)\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v}_{m1} = 5\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)\hat{i} - 5\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} - 3\hat{j}$$

The relative velocity of the rain with respect to the man is

$$\vec{v}_{rm1} = \vec{v}_r - \vec{v}_{m1}$$

$$\vec{v}_{rm1} = (v_x\hat{i} + v_y\hat{j}) - (4\hat{i} - 3\hat{j}) = (v_x - 4)\hat{i} + (v_y + 3)\hat{j}$$

It is given that the man observes the rain falling vertically downward, i.e., its horizontal component must equal zero:

$$v_x - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow v_x = 4\ \text{m/s}$$

Case 2 when moving up the inclined plane

The man now moving up the same incline at the same speed.

His new velocity vector ($\vec{v}_{m2}$) becomes:

$$\vec{v}_{m2} = -(5\cos 37^\circ)\hat{i} + (5\sin 37^\circ)\hat{j} = -4\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}$$

The new relative velocity of the rain with respect to the man is,

$$\vec{v}_{rm2} = \vec{v}_r - \vec{v}_{m2}$$

$$\vec{v}_{rm2} = (4\hat{i} + v_y\hat{j}) - (-4\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}) = (4 - (-4))\hat{i} + (v_y - 3)\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v}_{rm2} = 8\hat{i} + (v_y - 3)\hat{j}$$

It is given that this relative velocity vector makes an angle with the horizontal such that $\tan \theta = \frac{7}{8}$.

$$\tan \theta = \left|\frac{\text{Vertical Component}}{\text{Horizontal Component}}\right|$$

$$\frac{7}{8} = \frac{|v_y - 3|}{8}$$

$$|v_y - 3| = 7$$

Since rain falls downward toward the ground, $v_y$ must be a negative value.

$$-(v_y - 3) = 7 \Rightarrow 3 - v_y = 7 \Rightarrow v_y = -4\ \text{m/s}$$

So, the rain's actual velocity is $\vec{v} = v_x\hat{i} + v_y\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} - 4\hat{j}$. The speed of rain is,

$$v_r = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}$$

$$v_r = \sqrt{4^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 16} = \sqrt{32}\ \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the actual speed of the rain is $\sqrt{32}\ \text{m/s}$. Thus, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: Velocity of the river with respect to ground is given by $v_0$. Width of the river is $d$. A swimmer swims (with respect to water) perpendicular to the current with acceleration $a = 2t$ (where $t$ is time) starting from rest from the origin $O$ at $t=0$. The equation of trajectory of the path followed by the swimmer is
question: Velocity of the river with respect to ground is given by $v_0$. Width of the river is $d$. A swimmer swims (with respect to water) perpendicular to the current with acceleration $a = 2t$ (where $t$ is time) starting from rest from the origin $O$ at $t=0$. The equation of trajectory of the path followed by the swimmer is
solution:
The river flows horizontally with a constant velocity $v_0$ along the positive x-axis. The swimmer does not accelerate horizontally.

Velocity along the x-axis ($v_x$) is the rate of change of the x-coordinate with respect to time:

$$v_x = \frac{dx}{dt} = v_0$$

The position is $x = 0$ at $t = 0$:

$$\int\limits_{0}^{x} dx = \int\limits_{0}^{t} v_0\,dt$$

$$x = v_0 t$$

$$t = \frac{x}{v_0}$$

The swimmer moves perpendicular to the current (along the y-axis) with a time-dependent acceleration $a_y = 2t$.

$$a_y = \frac{dv_y}{dt} = 2t \rightarrow dv_y = 2t\,dt$$

Integrating from rest $v_y = 0$ at $t = 0$ to find the vertical velocity component at any time $t$:

$$\int\limits_{0}^{v_y} dv_y = \int\limits_{0}^{t} 2t\,dt$$

$$v_y = 2\left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{t} = t^2$$

As vertical velocity is the rate of change of the y-coordinate with respect to time:

$$v_y = \frac{dy}{dt} = t^2 \rightarrow dy = t^2\,dt$$

The vertical position is $y = 0$ at $t = 0$

$$\int\limits_{0}^{y} dy = \int\limits_{0}^{t} t^2 dt$$

$$y = \frac{t^3}{3}$$

Substitute time $\left(t = \frac{x}{v_0}\right)$,

$$y = \frac{1}{3} \left(\frac{x}{v_0}\right)^3$$

$$y = \frac{x^3}{3v_0^3}$$

Therefore, the equation of trajectory followed by the swimmer is $\frac{x^3}{3v_0^3}$. Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: A flag on a bus is fluttering in north direction & wind is blowing in east direction. Then which of the following will be true -
question: A flag on a bus is fluttering in north direction & wind is blowing in east direction. Then which of the following will be true -
options: bus is moving in north east direction.
bus may be moving in any direction between south & east.
bus may be moving in any direction between south & west.
solution:
Let the unit vector pointing east be $+ hd i$ and the unit vector along north be $+ hd j$

The wind blows due East, so velocity of the wind relative to the ground is $\vec{v}_W = v_W \hat{i}$

The flag on a moving vehicle flutters in the direction of the wind as experienced by the vehicle itself. Therefore,

the direction in which the flag flutters is the direction of the relative velocity of the wind with respect to the bus.

The flag flutters due north, so the relative velocity of the wind with respect to the bus is $\vec{v}_{W/B} = v_{\mathrm{rel}} \hat{j}$

$$\vec{v}_{W/B} = \vec{v}_W - \vec{v}_B$$

$$\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_W - \vec{v}_{W/B}$$

$$\vec{v}_B = (v_W \hat{i}) - (v_{\mathrm{rel}} \hat{j})$$

$$\vec{v}_B = v_W \hat{i} - v_{\mathrm{rel}} \hat{j}$$

The horizontal component is $+\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{W}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}$, which is towards the east.

The vertical component is $-\mathrm{v}_{\text {rel }} \hat{\mathrm{J}}$, which is towards the south.

Hence, the bus is moving in a direction somewhere between South and East.

Thus, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: A man who is wearing a hat of extended length of 12 cm is running in rain falling vertically downwards with speed 10 m/s. The maximum speed with which man can run, so that rain drops do not fall on his face (the length of his face below the extended part of the hat is 16 cm) will be :
question: A man who is wearing a hat of extended length of 12 cm is running in rain falling vertically downwards with speed 10 m/s. The maximum speed with which man can run, so that rain drops do not fall on his face (the length of his face below the extended part of the hat is 16 cm) will be :
solution:
Let the horizontal direction of the man's run be the positive x -axis $(+\hat{1})$ and the vertical direction pointing upwards be the positive y -axis $(+\hat{\mathrm{j}})$.

The rain falls vertically downwards at a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. So, the velocity of the rain relative to the ground is,

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{r}}=0 \hat{\imath}-10 \hat{\jmath}=-10 \hat{\jmath} $$

The man runs horizontally with an unknown maximum speed $v_m$. So, the velocity of the man relative to the ground is,

$$\vec{v}_m = v_m\hat{i}$$

The relative velocity of the rain with respect to the man is,

$$\vec{v}_{r/m} = \vec{v}_r - \vec{v}_m$$

$$\vec{v}_{r/m} = -10\hat{j} - v_m\hat{i} = -v_m\hat{i} - 10\hat{j}$$

In the frame of the man, the rain appears to approach at an angle $\theta$ with respect to the vertical axis.

$$\tan \theta = \frac{v_m}{10} ......{(i)}$$

The extended horizontal length of the hat's brim is $x = 12\ \text{cm}$.

The vertical length of his face extending directly below the edge of this brim is $y = 16\ \text{cm}$.

For the rain drops to completely clear his face without wetting it,

$$\tan \theta = \frac{x}{y}$$

$$\tan \theta = \frac{12}{16} = \frac{3}{4} \ldots \text{(ii)}$$

From both the equations,

$$\frac{v_m}{10} = \frac{3}{4}$$

$$v_m = 10 \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{30}{4} = \frac{15}{2}\ \text{m/s}$$

So, the maximum speed with which the man can run is $\frac{15}{2}\ \text{m/s}$.

Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: It is raining vertically downwards with a velocity of $3 \text{ km h}^{-1}$. A man walks in the rain with a velocity of $4 \text{ km h}^{-1}$. The rain drops will fall on the man with a relative velocity of :
question: It is raining vertically downwards with a velocity of $3 \text{ km h}^{-1}$. A man walks in the rain with a velocity of $4 \text{ km h}^{-1}$. The rain drops will fall on the man with a relative velocity of :
solution:
The rain falls vertically downwards with a speed of 3 km/h. In vector form, this is:

$$\vec{v}_r = -3\hat{\jmath}\ \mathrm{km/h}$$

The man walks horizontally with a speed of 4 km/h. In vector form, this is:

$$\vec{v}_m = 4\hat{\imath}\ \mathrm{km/h}$$

The velocity of rain as observed by frame of man is

$$\vec{v}_{r/m} = \vec{v}_r - \vec{v}_m$$

$$\vec{v}_{r/m} = (-3\hat{\jmath}) - (4\hat{\imath})$$

$$\vec{v}_{r/m} = (-4\hat{\imath} - 3\hat{\jmath})\ \mathrm{km/h}$$

Its net magnitude is,

$$\left|\vec{v}_{r/m}\right| = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}$$

$$\left|\vec{v}_{r/m}\right| = \sqrt{(-4)^2 + (-3)^2}$$

$$\left|\vec{v}_{r/m}\right| = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5\ \text{km/h}$$

Therefore, the relative velocity of the rain drops with respect to the man is $5\,\text{kmh}^{-1}$. Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: A boat, which has a speed of 5 km/h in still water, crosses a river of width 1 km along the shortest possible path in 15 minutes. The velocity of the river water in km/h is -
question: A boat, which has a speed of 5 km/h in still water, crosses a river of width 1 km along the shortest possible path in 15 minutes. The velocity of the river water in km/h is -
solution:
The velocity of the river water relative to the ground is directed purely along the x-axis with an unknown magnitude $v_r$:

$$\vec{v}_r = v_r\hat{i}$$

The boat's speed in still water represents its velocity relative to the water medium, with a given magnitude

$$v_{b/r} = 5\ \text{km/h}$$

The boat crosses the river along the shortest possible path. The shortest distance connecting two parallel banks is a straight perpendicular line directly across the river channel (along the y-axis).

Therefore, for the boat to stay on this straight track, its net resultant velocity vector relative to the ground ($\vec{v}_g$) must point purely along the y-axis, with zero horizontal drift along the x-axis:

$$\vec{v}_g = v_g\hat{j}$$

The velocity of the boat relative to the ground is $\vec{v}_g$ then,

$$\vec{v}_{b/r} = \vec{v}_g - \vec{v}_r$$

$$\Rightarrow \vec{v}_{b/r} = -v_r\hat{i} + v_g\hat{j}$$

The magnitude of velocity of boat with respect to river is,

$$|\vec{v}_{b/r}|^2 = v_r^2 + v_g^2$$

$$5^2 = v_r^2 + v_g^2 \rightarrow 25 = v_r^2 + v_g^2$$

For the uniform velocity is the total displacement divided by the time interval:

$$v_g = \frac{\text{Width of the river }(w)}{\text{Time taken }(t)}$$

The width of the river is $w = 1\ \text{km}$ and the time taken to cross it is $t = 15$ minutes.

$$t = \frac{15}{60}\ \text{hours} = \frac{1}{4}\ \text{hour} = 0.25\ \text{h}$$

So, $$v_g = \frac{1\ \text{km}}{1/4\ \text{h}} = 4\ \text{km/h}$$

From the magnitude of velocity of boat with respect to river,

$$25 = v_r^2 + 4^2$$

$$25 = v_r^2 + 16$$

$$v_r^2 = 25 - 16$$

$$v_r^2 = 9$$

$$v_r = \sqrt{9} = 3\ \text{km/h}$$

Therefore, the velocity of the river water is $3\ \text{km/h}$. Thus, the correct option is B.

subject: Physics
q: A man crosses the river perpendicular to river flow in time t seconds and travels an equal distance down the stream in T seconds. The ratio of man's speed in still water to the speed of river water will be :
question: A man crosses the river perpendicular to river flow in time t seconds and travels an equal distance down the stream in T seconds. The ratio of man's speed in still water to the speed of river water will be :
solution:
Let the river flow along the positive horizontal x -axis $(+\hat{i})$ and the perpendicular direction directly across the river width be along the positive vertical y -axis $(+\hat{\jmath})$.

Speed of the river water relative to the ground is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{r}} \hat{i}$

Speed of the man in still water is $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{m}}$

The man crosses the river strictly perpendicular to the river flow. This means his resultant velocity vector relative to the ground must have zero horizontal component and point completely across the width along the yaxis :

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{man/ground}} = 0\hat{i} + v_y\hat{j}$$

The man rows at an angle $\theta$ upstream against the current to prevent being swept down the river. His velocity vector relative to the water can resolved:

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{m/r}} = -v_m \sin\theta\,\hat{i} + v_m \cos\theta\,\hat{j}$$

The relative velocity of man with respect to ground is

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{man/ground}} = \vec{v}_{\mathrm{m/r}} + \vec{v}_r$$

$$\vec{v}_{\mathrm{man/ground}} = (-v_m \sin\theta + v_r)\hat{i} + (v_m \cos\theta)\hat{j}$$

For the motion to be along perfectly perpendicular,

$$-v_m \sin\theta + v_r = 0 \Rightarrow \sin\theta = \frac{v_r}{v_m}$$

Using trigonometric identity $\cos \theta = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta}$,

$$\cos \theta = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{v_r}{v_m}\right)^2}$$

Thus, his net perpendicular crossing speed $(v_y)$ relative to the bank is:

$$v_y = v_m \cos \theta = v_m \sqrt{1 - \frac{v_r^2}{v_m^2}} = \sqrt{v_m^2 - v_r^2}$$

Let the width of the river be d. The time t taken to cross this distance perpendicular to the bank is,

$$t = \frac{d}{\sqrt{v_m^2 - v_r^2}}$$

$$t^2 = \frac{d^2}{v_m^2 - v_r^2} \Rightarrow v_m^2 - v_r^2 = \frac{d^2}{t^2} \ldots (i)$$

When the man travels an equal distance d downstream in a time interval of T seconds.

$$v_{\text{downstream}} = v_m + v_r$$

The time T required to move this distance d downstream is:

$$T = \frac{d}{v_m + v_r}$$

$$T^2 = \frac{d^2}{(v_m + v_r)^2} \Rightarrow (v_m + v_r)^2 = \frac{d^2}{T^2}$$

$$v_m + v_r = \frac{d}{T} \ldots \text{(ii)}$$

On substituting the value of d from equation (ii) into equation (1):

$$v_m^2 - v_r^2 = \frac{[T(v_m + v_r)]^2}{t^2}$$

$$(v_m - v_r)(v_m + v_r) = \frac{T^2(v_m + v_r)^2}{t^2}$$

Since $v_m + v_r \neq 0$,

$$v_m - v_r = \frac{T^2}{t^2}(v_m + v_r)$$

$$t^2(v_m - v_r) = T^2(v_m + v_r)$$

$$t^2 v_m - t^2 v_r = T^2 v_m + T^2 v_r$$

$$t^2 v_m - T^2 v_m = t^2 v_r + T^2 v_r$$

$$v_m (t^2 - T^2) = v_r (t^2 + T^2)$$

$$\frac{v_m}{v_r} = \frac{t^2 + T^2}{t^2 - T^2}$$

Therefore, the ratio of the man's speed in still water to the river speed is $\frac{t^2 + T^2}{t^2 - T^2}$.

Thus, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: The river velocity is $5 \mathrm{ms^{-1}}$. A swimmer starts from point A and reaches point B after 2 s. The distance AB is $(8 + 6\sqrt{3})$ m. Find the velocity of swimmer with respect to river.
question: The river velocity is $5 \mathrm{ms^{-1}}$. A swimmer starts from point A and reaches point B after 2 s. The distance AB is $(8 + 6\sqrt{3})$ m. Find the velocity of swimmer with respect to river.
solution:
The velocity vector of the river flow is given as $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ downstream:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{r}}=51 \hat{\mathrm{~m}} / \mathrm{s} $$

The swimmer starts at point A and moves along a straight line path to land exactly at point B in a total time interval $t = 2 ext{ s}$. The displacement vector $\vec{s}$ from A to B makes an angle of $37^\circ$ with the horizontal river bank:

$$\vec{s} = s\cos(37^\circ)\hat{i} + s\sin(37^\circ)\hat{j}$$

It is given that the total distance is $s = (8 + 6\sqrt{3})\text{ m}$:

$$\vec{s} = (8 + 6\sqrt{3})\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)\hat{i} + (8 + 6\sqrt{3})\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)\hat{j}$$

Since the net velocity relative to the ground ($\vec{v}_g$) is constant over the straight line path:

$$\vec{v}_g = \frac{\vec{s}}{t} = \frac{\vec{s}}{2}$$

$$\vec{v}_g = (8 + 6\sqrt{3})\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)\hat{i} + (8 + 6\sqrt{3})(1.55)\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v}_g = \left(\frac{16 + 12\sqrt{3}}{5}\right)\hat{i} + \left(\frac{12 + 9\sqrt{3}}{5}\right)\hat{j}$$

The relative velocity of swimmer with respect to river is, $\vec{v}_{s/r} = \vec{v}_{sg} - \vec{v}_r$

$$\rightarrow \vec{v}_{sg} = \vec{v}_{s/r} + \vec{v}_r$$

$$\vec{v}_{s/r} = \vec{v}_g - \vec{v}_r$$

$$\vec{v}_{s/r} = \left[\left(\frac{16 + 12\sqrt{3}}{5}\right)\hat{i} + \left(\frac{12 + 9\sqrt{3}}{5}\right)\hat{j}\right] - 5\hat{i}$$

$$v_{x,s/r} = \frac{16 + 12\sqrt{3}}{5} - \frac{25}{5} = \frac{16 - 25 + 12\sqrt{3}}{5} = \frac{12\sqrt{3} - 9}{5}$$

$$v_{y,s/r} = \frac{12 + 9\sqrt{3}}{5}$$

So, the velocity of swimmer with respect to river is,

$$\vec{v}_{s/r} = \left(\frac{12\sqrt{3} - 9}{5}\right)\hat{i} + \left(\frac{9\sqrt{3} + 12}{5}\right)\hat{j}$$

The final speed is,

$$\left|\vec{v}_{s/r}\right| = \sqrt{v_{x,s/r}^{2} + v_{y,s/r}^{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\left(12\sqrt{3}-9\right)^{2}}{25} + \frac{\left(9\sqrt{3}+12\right)^{2}}{25}}$$

$$\left|\vec{v}_{s/r}\right| = \sqrt{\frac{(144 \times 3) - 2(12\sqrt{3})(9) + 81}{25} + \frac{(81 \times 3) + 2(9\sqrt{3})(12) + 144}{5}}$$

$$\left|\vec{v}_{s/r}\right| = \sqrt{\frac{(432 - 216\sqrt{3} + 81) + (243 + 216\sqrt{3} + 144)}{5}}$$

$$\left|\vec{v}_{s/r}\right| = \sqrt{\frac{900}{25}} = \frac{30}{5} = 6\text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the velocity of the swimmer with respect to the river is $6\text{ms}^{-1}$.

Thus, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: A boat is rowed across a river (perpendicular to river flow) at the rate of 9 km/hr. The river flows at the rate of 12 km/hr. The velocity of boat in km/hr is:
question: A boat is rowed across a river (perpendicular to river flow) at the rate of 9 km/hr. The river flows at the rate of 12 km/hr. The velocity of boat in km/hr is:
solution:
The river current flows at a rate of $12 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. Thus, the velocity vector of the river relative to the ground is:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{r}}=121 \hat{\mathrm{~km}} / \mathrm{h} $$

The boat is rowed perpendicular to the river flow at a rate of $9 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$.

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{b} / \mathrm{r}}=9 \hat{\jmath} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h} $$

The resultant velocity of the boat observed from the ground frame ($\vec{v}_g$) is the resultant vector of the boat's independent rowing motion through the water and the motion of the river current:

$$\vec{v}_g = \vec{v}_{b/r} + \vec{v}_r$$

$$\vec{v}_g = (12\hat{i} + 9\hat{j})\ \text{km/h}$$

So, the magnitude is,

$$|\vec{v}_g| = \sqrt{12^2 + 9^2}$$

$$|\vec{v}_g| = \sqrt{144 + 81}$$

$$|\vec{v}_g| = \sqrt{225}$$

$$|\vec{v}_g| = 15\ \text{km/h}$$

Therefore, the velocity of the boat is 15 km/h. Thus, the correct option is B.

subject: Physics
q: A man walks in rain with a velocity of $5\text{kmh}^{-1}$ The rain drops strike at him at an angle of $45^\circ$ with the horizontal. The velocity of the rain if it is falling vertically downward is :
question: A man walks in rain with a velocity of $5\text{kmh}^{-1}$ The rain drops strike at him at an angle of $45^\circ$ with the horizontal. The velocity of the rain if it is falling vertically downward is :
solution:
The man walks horizontally at a speed of $5 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{m}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h} $$

The rain is falling vertically downward relative to the ground.

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{r}}=-\mathrm{v} \hat{\mathrm{j}} $$

The velocity of the rain relative to the moving man is

$$\vec{v}_{r/m} = \vec{v}_r - \vec{v}_m$$

$$\vec{v}_{r/m} = -v\hat{j} - 5\hat{i}$$

$$\vec{v}_{r/m} = -5\hat{i} - v\hat{j}$$

From the man's frame of reference, the raindrops appear to strike him at an angle of $45^\circ$ relative to the horizontal ground.

$$\tan(45^\circ) = \frac{|-v|}{|-5|} = \frac{v}{5}$$

$$1 = \frac{v}{5}$$

$$v = 5\ \text{km/h}$$

Therefore, the velocity of the rain is $5\text{kmh}^{-1}$. Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: The velocity of swimmer $A$ and $B$ in still water is $5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. Swimmer $A$ decides to cross the river in minimum time and swimmer $B$ decides to cross the river through shortest path. They start from the same point simultaneously and at $t=t_0$, the distance of swimmer $A$ from the bank is 25 m and that of $B$ is 20 $\mathbf{m}$. Find speed of stream of river (in $\mathbf{m s}^{\mathbf{- 1}}$ ). (Assume the velocity of steam is lesser than the velocity of swimmer in water). Bank is from where they started.
question: The velocity of swimmer $A$ and $B$ in still water is $5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. Swimmer $A$ decides to cross the river in minimum time and swimmer $B$ decides to cross the river through shortest path. They start from the same point simultaneously and at $t=t_0$, the distance of swimmer $A$ from the bank is 25 m and that of $B$ is 20 $\mathbf{m}$. Find speed of stream of river (in $\mathbf{m s}^{\mathbf{- 1}}$ ). (Assume the velocity of steam is lesser than the velocity of swimmer in water). Bank is from where they started.
solution:
Velocity of swimmer in still water is $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

We need to find the velocity of the river stream.

Let $y_A, y_B=$ vertical displacement perpendicular to the bank at time $t_0$.

To cross a river in the minimum time, a swimmer must direct perpendicular to the river bank.

So, the velocity component across the river is $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{Ay}}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

Then distance from the bank at $\mathrm{t}_0$ will be,

$$ y_A=v_{A y} \times t_0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $25=5 \times \mathrm{t}_0$

$$ \mathrm{t}_0=5 \text { seconds } $$

To cross through the shortest path the swimmer must swim at an angle $\theta$ upstream so that the river's flow cancels out their horizontal movement.

So, the resultant velocity direction is perfectly perpendicular to the bank.

Thus, velocity component across the river is $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{By}}$

$$ v_{\mathrm{By}}=\sqrt{v_s^2-v_r^2} $$

So, distance from the bank at $\mathrm{t}_0=5 \mathrm{sec}$ is,

$$ y_B=v_{B y} \times t_0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $20=\sqrt{5^2-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{r}}^2} \times \mathrm{t}_0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $20=\sqrt{25-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{r}}^2} \times 5$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4=\sqrt{25-v_r^2}$

$\Rightarrow 4^2=25-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{r}}^2$

$\Rightarrow 16=25-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{r}}^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $v_r^2=25-16=9$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{r}}=\sqrt{9}=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

Therefore, the speed of the stream of the river is $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

subject: Physics
q: A boat which can move with a speed of 5 m/s relative to water crosses a river of width 480 m flowing with a constant speed of 4 m/s. What is the time taken by the boat to cross the river along the shortest path?
question: A boat which can move with a speed of 5 m/s relative to water crosses a river of width 480 m flowing with a constant speed of 4 m/s. What is the time taken by the boat to cross the river along the shortest path?
solution:
The constant speed of the flowing river water relative to the ground is $v_r = 4\ \mathrm{m/s}$. Its velocity vector is:

$$\vec{v}_r = 4\hat{i}\,\mathrm{m/s}$$

The boat's speed capacity in still water represents its velocity magnitude relative to the river medium, given as

$$v_{b/r} = 5\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

The total width of the river to be crossed is $w = 480\ \mathrm{m}$.

The boat must cross the river channel along its shortest path. The shortest distance connecting two parallel line banks is a straight line drawn perfectly perpendicular to them.

For the actual path of the boat relative to the fixed ground ($\vec{v}_g$) to remain straight along this cross-river line, it cannot have any horizontal drift downstream or upstream. Therefore, its net horizontal velocity component must equal zero:

$$\vec{v}_g = v_g\hat{\jmath}$$

The boat's relative velocity vector with respect to river is

$$\vec{v}_{b/r} = \vec{v}_g - \vec{v}_r$$

$$\vec{v}_{b/r} = -4\hat{\imath} + v_g\hat{\jmath}$$

Its magnitudes is,

$$\left|\vec{v}_{b/r}\right|^2 = (-4)^2 + v_g^2$$

$$5^2 = 16 + v_g^2$$

$$25 = 16 + v_g^2$$

$$v_g^2 = 25 - 16 = 9$$

$$v_g = \sqrt{9} = 3\ \text{m/s}$$

The crossing velocity $v_g$ is constant, the total time taken to cross the river is,

$$W = V_g \cdot T \Rightarrow T = \frac{W}{V_g}$$

$$T = \frac{480}{3} = 160 \text{ seconds}$$

Therefore, the time taken by the boat to cross the river along the shortest path is $160\ \text{s}$.

subject: Physics
q: A pipe is mounted on a cart. It can be rotated in the vertical plane. The cart moves uniformly along a horizontal path with a velocity of $2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. If at angle $$\theta $$ to the horizontal should the pipe be placed so that the rain drops falling vertically with a velocity of $6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ enter the pipe moving parallel to its wall. Then find the value of tan $$\theta $$
question: A pipe is mounted on a cart. It can be rotated in the vertical plane. The cart moves uniformly along a horizontal path with a velocity of $2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. If at angle $$\theta $$ to the horizontal should the pipe be placed so that the rain drops falling vertically with a velocity of $6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ enter the pipe moving parallel to its wall. Then find the value of tan $$\theta $$
solution:
For the rain to enter the pipe moving parallel to its walls, the pipe must be aligned along the direction in which the rain appears to fall as seen from the moving cart. In other words, the pipe should point along the velocity of rain relative to the cart.

From the cart's frame, the rain has two velocity components. The vertical component is the actual falling speed of the rain, which is $6 \text{ ms}^{-1}$ downward. The horizontal component is equal in size but opposite in direction to the cart's motion, so it is $2 \text{ ms}^{-1}$ pointing backward.

The angle $\theta$ that the pipe makes with the horizontal is found from the ratio of the vertical part to the horizontal part of this relative velocity.

$$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{vertical component}}{\text{horizontal component}} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$

So, $\tan \theta = 3$.

subject: Physics
q: A swimmer crosses the river along the line making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the direction of flow. Velocity of the river water is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Swimmer takes 12 seconds to cross the river of width 60 m . The velocity of the swimmer with respect to water will be :
question: A swimmer crosses the river along the line making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the direction of flow. Velocity of the river water is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Swimmer takes 12 seconds to cross the river of width 60 m . The velocity of the swimmer with respect to water will be :
solution:
The swimmer crosses a river of width $60\ \text{m}$ in $12\ \text{s}$.

So, the component of the swimmer’s velocity across the river is

$$ v_{\perp}=\frac{60}{12}=5\ \text{m/s} $$

Now, the swimmer moves along a line making an angle of $45^\circ$ with the direction of flow of the river.

This means the actual velocity of the swimmer with respect to the ground makes $45^\circ$ with the river flow.

So its component across the river is equal to its component along the river.

Hence, resultant velocity across the river $= 5\ \text{m/s}$, and along the river also $= 5\ \text{m/s}$.

Now, river water itself flows with velocity $5\ \text{m/s}$ along the river.

Let the swimmer’s velocity relative to water be $\vec{v}_{sw}$.

Then,

$$ \vec{v}_{sg}=\vec{v}_{sw}+\vec{v}_{wg} $$

where $\vec{v}_{sg}$ is swimmer’s velocity relative to ground and $\vec{v}_{wg}=5\ \text{m/s}$ along the flow.

Since the resultant velocity along the flow is also $5\ \text{m/s}$, the swimmer’s own velocity along the flow must be $0$.

Its entire velocity relative to water is only across the river, with magnitude $5\ \text{m/s}$.

So, the velocity of the swimmer with respect to water is

$$ \boxed{5\ \text{m/s}} $$

subject: Physics
q: While driving his scooter, a boy noticed that the raindrops strike him straight down with a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. If the scooter is moving along a level (horizontal) road with a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, determine the direction in which the rain is actually falling.
question: While driving his scooter, a boy noticed that the raindrops strike him straight down with a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. If the scooter is moving along a level (horizontal) road with a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, determine the direction in which the rain is actually falling.
solution:
As The boy rides the scooter, the rain appears to fall straight down on him with a speed of $10 \text{ ms}^{-1}$. This is the velocity of rain relative to the scooter, not the actual velocity of the rain.

The scooter itself is moving horizontally with a speed of $10 \text{ ms}^{-1}$. Let us take this direction as the positive horizontal direction $(\rightarrow)$.

The relation between the actual rain velocity and what the boy sees is:

$$ \vec{v}_{RS} = \vec{v}_R + (-\vec{v}_S) $$

Here, $\vec{v}_{RS}$ is the velocity of rain as seen by the boy (pointing straight down, $10 \text{ ms}^{-1}$), and $\vec{v}_S$ is the velocity of the scooter. To find the actual rain velocity $\vec{v}_R$, we add $\vec{v}_S$ back to $\vec{v}_{RS}$.

So the actual rain has two components — a downward part of $10 \text{ ms}^{-1}$ and a horizontal part of $10 \text{ ms}^{-1}$ (in the direction the scooter is moving).

The magnitude of the actual rain velocity is:

$$ v_R = \sqrt{10^2 + 10^2} = 10\sqrt{2} \text{ ms}^{-1} $$

To find the direction, we look at the angle the rain makes with the vertical:

$$ \tan\theta = \frac{10}{10} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 45^\circ $$

So the rain is actually falling at $10\sqrt{2} \text{ ms}^{-1}$, tilted at $45^\circ$ from the vertical, in the same horizontal direction as the scooter’s motion.
